summary: I was born in Tulsa, Oklahoma, in a house on 11th Street, right where the University of Tulsa is now. Growing up in Tulsa during the 1920s and 1930s, I remember going downtown with my parents to the Majestic Theater and having ice cream afterward. Tulsa was also the place where I witnessed the Tulsa race massacre in 1921 when I was eleven years old. It’s a city full of memories, both joyful and difficult.

Both my mother and father were of Creek descent, and I am mostly Creek myself. My family had allotments of about 160 acres each, which was common for Creek people at the time. I grew up knowing the struggles our people faced, especially with white settlers taking advantage of us by buying land for very little. Being Creek is a big part of who I am and my family’s history.

I have a little bit of Cherokee blood, but I’m really mostly Creek. The Perryman family, which is my mother’s family, are Creek, so that’s the primary heritage I identify with. Cherokee is part of my background, but it’s not as prominent as my Creek roots.

I went to grade school in Kindle, Oklahoma, and stayed there until the third grade. I believe Kindle is still there today, and it was my first school before I moved on to Sacred Heart Academy. It was a small town school that started my education.

After Kindle, I attended Sacred Heart Academy, a Catholic boarding and day school, from third through eighth grade. The school was run by Benedictine nuns who were very strict but kind to me. I lived at the boarding school and really liked it, and it was a big part of my early education.

The Benedictine nuns ran Sacred Heart Academy where I went to school. They were very strict but also very nice to me during my years there. I have always been connected to them and remember their discipline and care fondly.

I graduated from Monte Cassino High School, which was the high school connected to Sacred Heart Academy. My graduating class was very small, only two students including me. I remember one classmate’s father was a judge in Tulsa, but I can’t recall her name now.

As a child in Tulsa, my family and I would go downtown to see movies at the Majestic Theater. Before the movies, they often had stage shows with dancing, singing, and playing. It was a special treat and a fond memory from my youth.

entity: Bob Wills and His Texas Playboys
summary: My mother was a big fan of country music, especially Bob Wills and His Texas Playboys. She loved to square dance to their music, though I never learned to square dance myself. Their music was part of the soundtrack of my childhood in Tulsa.

Cain’s Ballroom was a place my mother frequented for square dancing and country music. I was too young to go with her, but I remember hearing about it. The building was originally built in 1924 as a garage for W. Tate Brady’s automobiles and still stands today as a music venue.

W. Tate Brady was a man whose automobiles garage was the original purpose of the building that later became Cain’s Ballroom in Tulsa. The building was built in 1924 for his garage. I remember this bit of Tulsa history because Cain’s Ballroom is still a well-known place.

I was eleven years old during the Tulsa race massacre in 1921. I remember seeing trucks with men standing in them, probably National Guard troops, and some smoke and fire in the distance. My mother was very brave and took a black man and his wife who worked for us to safety by back roads. It was a frightening time, but I felt protected because of my mother’s strength.

I was born at 11th and Delaware in Tulsa, which is now where the University of Tulsa is located. That area has changed a lot since my childhood. It’s interesting to think that the place of my birth is now a university campus.

I was alive during the Wall Street Crash of 1929 and the Great Depression that followed. However, I didn’t realize it was a depression because my family was already poor before then. We struggled with hunger sometimes, but my mother worked as a cook and we had a garden to help get by.

My husband’s name was Marvin E. Ashbaugh, and we married in 1934 when I was twenty-four. He was a pianist, composer, and orchestra leader who worked as a staff pianist for Walt Disney and even played for the Mouseketeers. We were married for thirty-four years, and though he was younger than me, we had a strong bond.

I met my husband Marvin at Holy Family Catholic Church in Tulsa. We would both hurry up the steps to get to the late mass, and that’s where we started talking and eventually married. The church was an important place in our lives.

My husband worked as a pianist for Walt Disney and I met Walt Disney once. He was very nice, though I didn’t know him well. My husband also traveled with the Mouseketeers as their pianist, which was quite an experience.

My husband played piano for the Mouseketeers and traveled with them during their shows. I didn’t travel with him, as I worked full time as a secretary. It was impressive that he was part of that famous group’s musical accompaniment.

I worked as an executive secretary for many years, mostly for a company called Chestnut and Smith. I stayed in the workforce until I was ninety-six years old, often working with people much younger than me. Working kept me young and active, and I needed the money too.
